text: Modulating the surface strain of nanoparticles (NPs)—the compression or expansion of surface atoms—is a powerful strategy for tuning surface chemistry and optimizing catalytic performance. In this study, we demonstrate that the surface strain of Pt in core/shell FePt/Pt NPs (featuring three atomic layers of Pt) can be precisely controlled by inducing a structural phase transition in the core from a cubic solid solution (fcc) to a tetragonal intermetallic (fct) structure. Quantum mechanics-molecular mechanics (QM-MM) simulations suggest that the significantly enhanced activity of fct-FePt/Pt NPs toward the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) stems from the relaxation of overcompressed Pt strain facilitated by the fct-FePt core. We further optimized this strain effect by partially substituting Fe with Cu; the resulting fct-FeCuPt/Pt NPs emerged as an exceptionally efficient catalyst, delivering a specific activity nearly ten times higher than that of commercial Pt. This approach of structure-induced surface strain control provides a versatile platform for engineering highly efficient catalysts for the ORR and a broad range of other chemical transformations.
